items.headline: Discovering a Crucial Signaling Pathway Connecting Liver Cancer and Fibrosis
items.preview: A study reveals how liver tumors promote fibrosis, accelerating cancer progression.
items.article:
Researchers at the Institute of Science Tokyo have identified the SPP1–CD44–Hedgehog signaling axis as a key driver of intratumoral fibrosis in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). This discovery highlights the interplay between liver tumors and fibrotic tissue, which contributes to cancer aggressiveness and treatment resistance.

Why this matters: Understanding this molecular mechanism opens new avenues for therapeutic interventions aimed at disrupting the tumor-stroma interactions in liver cancer, potentially improving patient outcomes. The findings suggest that targeting this signaling pathway could mitigate fibrosis-related drug resistance and enhance survival rates in HCC patients.

items.headline: Sarepta tests new Elevidys safeguards; Sana advances diabetes cell therapy
items.preview: Sarepta Therapeutics evaluates safeguards for Elevidys after liver failure incidents.
items.article:
Sarepta Therapeutics is testing new safeguards to reduce liver damage risks associated with its gene therapy Elevidys for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Following acute liver failure incidents in two patients, the company is enrolling 25 patients in a study to assess a regimen involving sirolimus.

Meanwhile, Sana Biotechnology reported positive results from its experimental cell therapy for Type 1 diabetes, showing sustained insulin production in a patient 14 months post-treatment. The company plans to seek regulatory approval for a Phase 1 trial of a new stem-cell derived therapy.

items.headline: 3PBIOVIAN and INC partner to expand access to oncology treatments in Colombia
items.preview: 3PBIOVIAN collaborates with INC to develop a biosimilar monoclonal antibody for cancer.
items.article:
3PBIOVIAN has partnered with the National Cancer Institute in Colombia to develop a biosimilar monoclonal antibody aimed at improving access to oncology treatments in the country. This initiative marks a significant step in local biomanufacturing and aims to reduce reliance on imported biologics.

The collaboration will cover the entire development process, from cell line creation to technology transfer for manufacturing. This project is expected to enhance patient access to effective cancer therapies in Colombia.

items.headline: Impact Journals to Participate in AACR Annual Meeting 2026 in San Diego
items.preview: Impact Journals announces participation in the AACR Annual Meeting to showcase research.
items.article:
Impact Journals will exhibit at the AACR Annual Meeting 2026 in San Diego, highlighting its commitment to advancing oncology and biomedical sciences. The event will gather global cancer researchers and clinicians to discuss scientific breakthroughs.

Visitors to their booth will engage with the editorial team and explore recent impactful publications, fostering collaborations and discussions on emerging trends in cancer research and publishing innovations.

items.headline: Eye movements reflect memory-related theta activity in the human brain
items.preview: Study reveals how eye movements influence memory-related brain activity during navigation.
items.article:
A study published in PLOS Biology demonstrates that theta oscillations in the human medial temporal lobe are significantly linked to saccadic eye movements during memory-guided navigation. The research indicates that these eye movements enhance memory performance and are coupled with exploratory visual search.

This finding provides insights into the mechanisms by which eye movements may organize mnemonic computations, highlighting the importance of theta dynamics in memory processes.
